title: A teszteredmények társadalmi egyenlőtlensége és az általános iskolai szegregáció
summary_en: Has primary-school segregation increased in Hungary, and is it widening test-score gaps? Hermann uses 2006-2019 data to track both school-level segregation and the social-background test-score gradient. School segregation by social background grew substantially over the period, with particularly strong increases for the bottom quintile of the income distribution. Test-score gaps by social background also widened during the second half of the window. Crucially, the two move together: districts and cities with stronger school segregation also have larger test-score gaps. The findings are consistent with school-level peer-and-resource effects driving social-mobility-linked outcomes.
data_used: Hungarian National Competence Test (kompetenciamérés) results 2006-2019, linked to family-background indicators and school-level demographics. Segregation measured at school level using standard segregation indices.
policy_relevance: Direct relevance to Hungarian education policy debates (KIM, EMMI). The paper provides causal-style evidence that the post-2010 educational-policy framework (free school choice + expansion of church-run and 6/8-year selective programmes) has increased segregation and widened test-score gaps. Useful empirical input for the integration-vs-selection policy debate and for any future segregation-monitoring requirement under EU education-policy frameworks.
